title: Mostaccioli
subtitle: Neapolitan spiced chocolate cookies with dark chocolate glaze
excerpt: Mostaccioli are a Neapolitan Christmas cookie: a dense, spiced chocolate biscuit shaped into rough lozenges, baked until firm, and dipped in dark chocolate while warm so the glaze sets glossy and thin. They smell strongly of the spices (cinnamon, cloves, and pepper) and the chocolate is not sweet. They are robust enough to last for several weeks in a tin, and in Naples they are made in advance and given as gifts. They improve substantially after resting for 3 days.
sourceNotes: Mostaccioli are among the oldest surviving Christmas sweets of Italy, with references to spiced biscuits of this type in Neapolitan and Calabrian cookery dating to the sixteenth century. The name derives from mosto, grape must, which was historically used to sweeten them. In the modern version honey replaces the must. They appear in Ippolito Cavalcanti's 'Cucina Teorico-Pratica' (1837), the first major cookbook of Neapolitan cuisine.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before glazing. Glaze after thawing at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The flavour is consistent at scale.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 4 to 6 weeks. The flavour deepens significantly after 3 days.

content.text: The dough is dark, fragrant, and dense; mixed by hand and shaped into rough diamond or lozenge shapes without a cutter. It does not need to be neat; the irregular surface catches the chocolate glaze. The biscuit must be firm all the way through before glazing: a soft centre means the glaze will slide or the cookie will sag. Dip while still warm from the oven so the chocolate spreads easily. Rest for 24 hours minimum before eating; freshly baked mostaccioli taste raw and sharp. After 3 days, they are something else entirely.

Warm 100 g honey until just runny. Combine 300 g flour, 40 g cocoa, 80 g ground almonds, 80 g sugar, 60 g chopped mixed peel, 1.5 tsp cinnamon, 0.25 tsp ground cloves, 0.25 tsp allspice, and 0.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda. Add the warm honey and 2 beaten eggs. Mix to a firm, somewhat stiff dough. If it is too dry to come together, add a tablespoon of warm water. On a floured surface, roll to about 8 mm thickness. Cut into diamond shapes about 5 × 3 cm. Place on lined baking trays. Bake at 175 degrees C fan for 12 to 15 minutes until firm throughout. They will not colour much; check firmness rather than colour.

Melt 200 g dark chocolate over a bain-marie until smooth. Working while the biscuits are still warm from the oven, dip each one fully in the chocolate, let the excess drip off, and place on a rack over a tray. The chocolate sets to a thin, firm coating. Leave at room temperature for 24 hours before storing in a tin.

items.problem: Dough is too dry and crumbly
items.solution: The honey was too thick or did not fully incorporate. Warm it until properly runny. Add warm water a tablespoon at a time until the dough holds together when pressed.

items.problem: Chocolate glaze is too thick and uneven
items.solution: The chocolate cooled before the dipping was complete. Work quickly once the chocolate is melted and keep the bowl over warm (not simmering) water to maintain its fluidity throughout.

items.problem: Biscuits taste raw and sharp right after baking
items.solution: This is expected. Mostaccioli must rest for at least 24 hours, ideally 3 days, for the spices to mellow and integrate. Do not taste-test until day two.
